title: Dostoevsky — The Threshold in the Human Soul
subtitle: The underground man trapped in the kañcukas, Raskolnikov's crime and redemption as the karmic cycle, the Grand Inquisitor as the false guru, Myshkin's epileptic aura as the threshold experience

summary.one_line: Dostoevsky's major works read through the Śaiva lens of the kañcukas (the sheaths of limitation), the karmic cycle of contraction and expansion, the false guru, and the threshold experience — where...
summary.scope: Covers Notes from Underground, Crime and Punishment, The Idiot, and The Brothers Karamazov (the latter treated in a dedicated RO). Focus on the threshold experiences, the contraction of consciousness, and the daimonic encounters that structure each novel's spiritual arc.

sources.label: Dostoevsky, Fyodor — The Brothers Karamazov (1880) [preliminary, see dedicated RO]

body.text: [See Notes from Underground throughout. The underground man is the soul trapped in the kañcukas — the five sheaths of limitation that Śaiva theology describes as the contractions of the infinite into the finite. His hyperconsciousness is the disease of the bound soul: he sees every chain that binds him and cannot break a single one. The 'wall' he rails against is kāla (limited agency) — the brute facticity of 2+2=4 that the ego cannot transcend. His spite (mala) is ahaṃkāra (the I-sense) turned pathological: he knows his degradation and clings to it as identity. He is the śakti-nipāta stalled — the descent of grace that awakens the soul to its bondage but does not (yet) liberate it. The underground is the space between the impure and the pure: the soul that knows it is bound but does not yet know how to unbind.]

[See Crime and Punishment throughout. Raskolnikov's murder of the pawnbroker is the karmic act that initiates a cycle of contraction: the crime (the extreme assertion of the separated I — 'Am I a trembling creature or have I the right?') generates guilt (the weight of the act pressing on the subtle body), isolation (the cutting of the links between the self and the other), and the slow unwinding of the consequence through suffering. His redemption is not forensic but existential: it requires confession (the reversal of the contraction through self-exposure), the acceptance of punishment (tapas — the heat of suffering that burns the karma), and the gradual restoration of relationship (through Sonia, who functions as the daimon leading him across the threshold). The epilogue in Siberia is the turning: the moment of resurrection where the karma is exhausted and the soul begins to expand again. The entire arc is the micro-cycle of bandha (bondage), bhoga (experience), and mokṣa (liberation).]

[See The Idiot Part 2 ch.5. Prince Myshkin's epileptic aura is the threshold experience par excellence — the moment before the seizure when consciousness. He describes it: 'There was a moment or two in his epileptic condition almost before the fit itself … when suddenly, in the midst of sadness, spiritual darkness and oppression, his brain seemed to catch fire at brief moments … His sensation of being alive and his awareness increased tenfold at those moments … his mind and heart were flooded with a dazzling light.' This is the glimpse of the para-samvit — the pure consciousness that lies behind the veil of the ordinary mind. The aura is the threshold opening: the vaikharī level parting to reveal the madhyama, the madhyama parting to reveal the paśyantī. But the seizure follows — the body cannot sustain the intensity of the opening. Myshkin's tragedy is that he lives at the threshold without being able to cross it.]

[See The Brothers Karamazov Book 5 ch.5. The Grand Inquisitor is the false guru — the one who claims to love humanity but despises the human soul. He rejects Christ (the true guru, the daimon) because Christ gave humans freedom, and freedom is unbearable. The false guru offers bread (the satisfaction of material needs), miracle (the suspension of natural law that impresses the unawakened), and authority (the removal of the burden of choice). These are the three bonds of the kañcukas: kāla (limited agency, replaced by bread as immediate satisfaction), niyati (causality, replaced by miracle as arbitrary intervention), and rāga (attachment, replaced by authority as the surrender of the will). The Inquisitor's silence — Christ says nothing — is the guru's ultimate response: the true guru does not argue with the false; the true guru simply stands there, present, not needing to prove. The kiss is the śaktipāta (the transmission of grace) that the false guru cannot receive.]

[See Crime and Punishment Parts 4-6 and Epilogue. Sonya Marmeladova is the daimon in human form — the threshold figure who leads Raskolnikov across the divide. She is a prostitute (the extreme of social impurity) who reads the Gospel to the murderer (the extreme of spiritual impurity). She does not preach; she simply is with him. She is the one who tells him to go to the crossroads and kiss the earth — the act of prostration before the gross that opens the passage to the subtle. She follows him to Siberia not as a lover but as a presence, a function. The moment of resurrection at the very end — 'she was everything to him' — is not romantic but daimonic: the soul recognising the guide who has been beside it throughout. Sonya is the śakti that descends when the soul has exhausted its own resources.]

[See The Idiot throughout and The Brothers Karamazov (Father Zosima). Dostoevsky's holy fools — Myshkin, Sonya, Alyosha, Zosima — are the vidyeśvaras: the lords of knowledge who carry the transmission of grace not through doctrine but through presence. They are mad by the standards of the world because they live from a level the world cannot perceive. Myshkin's 'idiocy' is the refusal to accept the world's version of reality — the refusal of the kañcukas' claim to be the whole. The holy fool is the vidyeśvara who has returned from the threshold and speaks from the other side: incomprehensible to the bound soul, yet strangely irresistible. Dostoevsky's genius is to show that the vidyeśvara cannot save the world — Myshkin fails, Rogozhin kills Nastasya, Myshkin regresses into idiocy — because the vidyeśvara's function is to show the threshold, not to carry everyone across it.]

gaps: Full mapping of each kancuka to a specific underground man passage needed
The karmic mechanics of Raskolnikov's dream-cycle not yet analysed
Myshkin's four dreams as four levels of threshold opening
Full treatment deferred to the Brothers Karamazov dedicated RO

issues.title: All passages are meta-commentary — need primary text extraction
issues.description: Passages currently describe the esoteric mapping. Real passages should include actual quotations from the novels with analysis.

issues.title: Demons / The Possessed not covered
issues.description: Dostoevsky's Demons contains the most explicit treatment of possession — the daimon as the invading force that takes over the soul. Stavrogin as the soul that cannot choose between contraction and expansion. Not yet integrated.

issues.title: The Dream of a Ridiculous Man not covered
issues.description: This short story contains the most direct depiction of a threshold crossing and the vision of a perfect world before the fall. A crucial passage for the daimonic encounter.

versions.summary: Initial RO created mapping Dostoevsky's major works onto the Śaiva framework of kañcukas, karma, threshold experience, and daimonic encounter. Six passages covering the underground man, Raskolnikov's karma, Myshkin's aura, the Grand Inquisitor, Sonya as daimon, and the holy fool as vidyeśvara.

timeline.event: RO created in draft state with 4 primary sources and 6 passages
